toolUseResult.originalFile:
==== Decision

Cloud Build configuration files for trigger-based builds use `cloudbuild.json` format,
generated programmatically via `jq`.
YAML is not used.

==== Rationale

JSON is the Cloud Build REST API's native wire format.
The `projects.builds.create` and `projects.triggers.run` endpoints accept JSON request bodies
modeled on the API's `Build` resource.
YAML is a file-authoring convenience that gets parsed into the same `Build` structure before submission.

Programmatic generation via `jq` produces correct JSON directly — no YAML indentation
fragility, no multiline string quoting hazards, no dependency on `yq` or custom emitters.
The stitch function (`zrbf_stitch_build_json`) already generates JSON;
converting to YAML would add complexity with zero functional benefit.

==== Evidence

[cols="1,3",options="header"]
|===
|Claim
|Source

|REST API is natively JSON
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/api/reference/rest/v1/projects.builds/create[projects.builds.create API reference] — request body is a `Build` resource in JSON

|YAML and JSON are equivalent serializations
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/build-config-file-schema[Build configuration file schema] — "You can write the build config file using the YAML or the JSON syntax"

|Triggers accept both formats
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/automating-builds/create-manage-triggers[Create and manage build triggers] — "Cloud Build configuration file (yaml or json)"

|Trigger autodetect precedence includes JSON
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/api/reference/rest/v1/projects.triggers[projects.triggers REST resource] — precedence: `cloudbuild.yaml`, `cloudbuild.yml`, `cloudbuild.json`, `Dockerfile`

|No deprecation signals through Feb 2026
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/release-notes[Cloud Build release notes] — no mention of reducing JSON support

|SLSA provenance does not differ by format
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/securing-builds/generate-validate-build-provenance[Generate and validate build provenance] — provenance records `buildConfigSource.path` (file name), not format-specific fields
|===

==== What Dies

* Any assumption that `cloudbuild.yaml` is required for triggers or SLSA provenance
* The linked term `rbtgr_build_yaml` — replaced by
{rbtgr_build_json}
* YAML-specific concerns: multiline string quoting, indentation sensitivity, `$$` escaping conventions

==== What Survives

* `zrbf_stitch_build_json()` — continues to emit JSON via `jq`, adapted for trigger structure
* `rbgjb/*.sh` step scripts — inlined into JSON `args` arrays at inscribe time
* All `_RBGY_*` substitution variables — baked into JSON `substitutions` object
* OCI Layout Bridge pattern — unchanged

==== Provenance Note

SLSA v1.0 provenance records `buildConfigSource.path` which will contain `cloudbuild.json`.
This is a cosmetic difference from `cloudbuild.yaml` — the structured provenance fields
(build inputs, parameters, outputs) are identical regardless of config file format.

==== References

* Research conducted: 2026-02-24 (web search + API documentation review)
* Google docs consistently present YAML examples by convention, not by requirement
* The `gcloud builds submit` CLI internally converts any config format to JSON for API submission

==== Capture Composition Contract (rblds_ spine)

This section governs the host-composed `projects.builds.create` path for Lode
captures — distinct from the trigger-autodetect path the Decision above covers.
A Lode capture is composed on the host by the capture-assembly spine
(`rblds_Spine.sh`) from two data inputs handed by a per-kind body (e.g. bole's
`zrbld_ensconce_submit`): an ordered _recipe_ of step rows and an opaque
_substitutions_ blob. The spine carries no per-kind branch and no
kind-registration mechanism — a new Lode kind is a new body file the spine
consumes as data, never a conditional inside the spine.

===== Spine/body boundary

[cols="1,3",options="header"]
|===
|Owner |Carries

|Spine (`rblds_`)
|The step-composition loop (recipe rows -> `steps[]`); the Build-resource
envelope shape (`serviceAccount`, `options.automapSubstitutions`,
`options.logging`, `options.pool`, `timeout`); submit + build-id persist +
console link; the poll via `zrbfc_wait_build_completion`; and the
`buildStepOutputs[N]` base64-decode. The capture-domain constants — the mason
capture SA, the `TETHER` pool, the regime build timeout — are spine-owned,
because every Lode capture fetches upstream bytes as mason over the network.

|Body (`rbld«kind»_`)
|The recipe rows (pre-resolved); the substitutions blob; the poll ceiling (the
one kind-flavored build knob); and the interpretation of the decoded step
output (the per-member envelope fan-out).
|===

The only kind-flavored build knob the body passes is the poll ceiling (ensconce
borrows the enshrine ceiling). `serviceAccount`/`pool`/`timeout` are NOT body
parameters: they are capture-domain invariants the spine owns. A future kind
that needed a different pool or SA would change the spine's capture-domain
constants deliberately, not slip past as recipe data.

===== Recipe row format

A recipe is an ordered list of `|`-delimited step rows. The `|` delimiter is
load-bearing: builder image refs carry colons (tags and `sha256:` digests), so a
colon delimiter would be ambiguous.

----
script_path | builder_image | id | entrypoint
----

[cols="1,3",options="header"]
|===
|Field |Meaning

|`script_path`   |Absolute path to the step script, _pre-resolved by the body_. The spine owns no steps-directory knowledge.
|`builder_image` |The step's builder image ref (a reliquary tool image).
|`id`            |The Cloud Build step id.
|`entrypoint`    |`bash` \| `sh` \| `python3` — selects the composed shebang line. Any other value fails composition.
|===

The spine reads each script's body (shebang stripped), prepends the shebang the
entrypoint selects, and inlines it as the step's `script` field. This is the
same shape the made-side `rbfca_` assemblers use; the capture spine is the
canonical single copy for the fetched side.

===== Substitutions blob — opaque

The body composes the substitutions object (the `_RBGL_*` keys) into a file; the
spine slots it verbatim into the Build resource's `substitutions` field and
reads no key from it. `options.automapSubstitutions` maps the keys into each
step's environment. The spine's opacity to the blob is what keeps it
registration-agnostic — a new kind authors a different blob without touching the
spine.

Invariant (RBSCB): substitutions and `/workspace` carry non-secret data only;
secrets are step-local and re-minted.

===== Extract contract

The spine's extract decodes one step's `buildStepOutputs[N]` slot — base64 to a
destination file — where `N` is the only generic parameter. The decoded JSON's
shape (for bole: `slot_1`..`slot_N` member envelopes, each a stamp + vouch) is
the body's knowledge; the spine is member-count-agnostic. This keeps the
N-member envelope axis out of the spine.

===== Requires/provides format (designed here; enforced by the recipe validator)

The recipe row above is the format implemented today. The capture domain also
needs a per-step _requires/provides_ declaration so a dispatch-time validator
can reject a bad composition before the expensive build. The FORMAT is fixed
here; its enforcement is the recipe-validator pace.

A step composes over two register channels:

* _Substitution registers_ — `_RBGL_*` keys present in the substitutions blob,
  automapped into the step environment.
* _/workspace registers_ — non-secret files a step writes for a later step,
  named by path relative to `/workspace`.

Each step declares two token lists:

* `requires` — substitution-register keys and `/workspace` register paths the
  step reads before it runs.
* `provides` — `/workspace` register paths the step writes (and, for the
  terminal capture step, the `buildStepOutputs` slot it authors).

The validator walks the recipe in order: every `requires` token must be
satisfied by either a key present in the substitutions blob or a `provides`
token from an earlier step. An unsatisfiable `requires` fails dispatch with a
named-token diagnostic, before submit.

The format is deliberately shaped by the capture domain's three axes:

* _N-member envelopes_ — the terminal step's `provides` is the single
  `buildStepOutputs` slot, not per-member registers; member fan-out is body
  knowledge and stays out of the step contract.
* _Tool variety_ — registers are declared identically regardless of builder

  contract vocabulary.
* _Opaque-blob payloads_ — `requires` references substitution keys by NAME; the
  validator checks key _presence_, never value shape, preserving the spine's
  opacity to the blob.

input.old_string:
* _Opaque-blob payloads_ — `requires` references substitution keys by NAME; the
  validator checks key _presence_, never value shape, preserving the spine's

input.new_string:
* _Opaque-blob payloads_ — `requires` references substitution keys by NAME; the
  validator checks key _presence_, never value shape, preserving the spine's
  opacity to the blob.

===== Composed-snippet library (rbgjs)

Cloud Build offers no native step reuse. Where the same in-step logic recurs
across cloud steps, it is authored once as a _snippet_ in the shared cloud-step
home `Tools/rbk/rbgjs/` and stitched into each step at host-assembly time. The
home deliberately breaks the `rbgj«family»` letter scheme — a shared snippet
belongs to no single made-side family, so `s` is the no-family family.

*Mechanism.* A step marks an inclusion point with a directive line

----
#@rbgjs_include «name»
----

(any leading indentation tolerated). At assembly, `zrbfc_expand_includes`
(`rbfcb_BuildHost.sh`, sibling to `zrbfc_write_script_body`, sourced by both the
Lode spine and the made-side `rbfca_` vouch assembler) rewrites the step body,
splicing in `rbgjs-«name».sh` with its leading shebang stripped. A body with no
marker is rewritten unchanged, so an assembler may call the expander for every
step. An unknown `«name»` fails the expander, which fails dispatch before submit
(crash-fast — no silent skip). The snippet shebang exists only so shellcheck
lints the fragment as bash; it never reaches the build.

*Register channel — shell vars, not substitutions.* A snippet reads the plain
upper-case shell vars the kind sets immediately before the marker, and writes
plain shell vars the kind reads after — it is blind to `_RBGx_` substitution
names. This is load-bearing: it is precisely what lets one snippet serve callers
whose substitution namespaces are disjoint (e.g. Lode's `_RBGL_*` and
hallmark-verify's `_RBGV_*`). The substitution-register channel (automapped
`_RBGx_` keys) and the snippet shell-var channel are distinct; a snippet never
crosses into the substitution channel.

*The library (each snippet's requires/provides):*

[cols="1,3",options="header"]
|===
|Snippet |Contract

|`token-fetch`        |_requires_ none (ambient metadata server); _provides_ `TOKEN`. The canonical skopeo-to-GAR metadata-server token (RBSCB), in-memory, never to `/workspace`.
|`skopeo-fingerprint` |_requires_ `ORIGIN`, `RAW_FILE`; _provides_ `SHA`, `FINGERPRINT`. Inspect the raw manifest, take its canonical sha256, derive the sanitized-origin glance fingerprint.
|`buildx-bootstrap`   |_requires_ none; _provides_ the `rb-builder` buildx builder, created-if-absent and selected. Idempotent under retry; run once per step.
|`buildx-push`        |_requires_ `PUSH_URI`, `PUSH_PLATFORMS`, `PUSH_CTX` (and the bootstrap builder); _provides_ the image pushed to `PUSH_URI`. The irreducible FROM-scratch push.
|===

*Share the primitive, not the whole step.* The vouch-push step (Lode `rbgjl02`,
hallmark-verify `rbgjv03`) is NOT one shared step: the two diverge on five
load-bearing axes — push cardinality (loop-N members vs single), destination-URI
template and arity, platform source (literal `linux/amd64` vs a `/workspace`
platforms file), a conditional Dockerfile (conjure verify files), and disjoint
substitution sets. Only the buildx bootstrap and the push invocation are
identical, so only those are shared — as `buildx-bootstrap` + `buildx-push`.
Cardinality, URI construction, platform choice, and context assembly (which JSON,
which Dockerfile) stay in the per-kind step. The same boundary governs
`skopeo-fingerprint`: the inspect→digest→fingerprint core is identical across
captures, but the first GAR copy is excluded because its destination tag is
itself digest-derived and kind-specific, so it cannot be a clean shared
parameter. The rule the library follows: share the irreducible identical core,
leave kind-specific assembly in the kind.

*In-heat reach (partial dedup, forward payoff).* `buildx-bootstrap`/`buildx-push`
have two live callers (`rbgjl02`, `rbgjv03`). `token-fetch` and
`skopeo-fingerprint` have one live caller each (bole's `rbgjl01`) this heat — the
other copy lives in enshrine (`rbgje01`), which is reference-not-migrated, so its
fork stays until that cutover. The snippets are authored once now regardless: the
library teaches "compose from the library," and the dedup completes when the
doomed forks are removed.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
==== Decision

Cloud Build configuration files for trigger-based builds use `cloudbuild.json` format,
generated programmatically via `jq`.
YAML is not used.

==== Rationale

JSON is the Cloud Build REST API's native wire format.
The `projects.builds.create` and `projects.triggers.run` endpoints accept JSON request bodies
modeled on the API's `Build` resource.
YAML is a file-authoring convenience that gets parsed into the same `Build` structure before submission.

Programmatic generation via `jq` produces correct JSON directly — no YAML indentation
fragility, no multiline string quoting hazards, no dependency on `yq` or custom emitters.
The stitch function (`zrbf_stitch_build_json`) already generates JSON;
converting to YAML would add complexity with zero functional benefit.

==== Evidence

[cols="1,3",options="header"]
|===
|Claim
|Source

|REST API is natively JSON
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/api/reference/rest/v1/projects.builds/create[projects.builds.create API reference] — request body is a `Build` resource in JSON

|YAML and JSON are equivalent serializations
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/build-config-file-schema[Build configuration file schema] — "You can write the build config file using the YAML or the JSON syntax"

|Triggers accept both formats
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/automating-builds/create-manage-triggers[Create and manage build triggers] — "Cloud Build configuration file (yaml or json)"

|Trigger autodetect precedence includes JSON
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/api/reference/rest/v1/projects.triggers[projects.triggers REST resource] — precedence: `cloudbuild.yaml`, `cloudbuild.yml`, `cloudbuild.json`, `Dockerfile`

|No deprecation signals through Feb 2026
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/release-notes[Cloud Build release notes] — no mention of reducing JSON support

|SLSA provenance does not differ by format
|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/securing-builds/generate-validate-build-provenance[Generate and validate build provenance] — provenance records `buildConfigSource.path` (file name), not format-specific fields
|===

==== What Dies

* Any assumption that `cloudbuild.yaml` is required for triggers or SLSA provenance
* The linked term `rbtgr_build_yaml` — replaced by
{rbtgr_build_json}
* YAML-specific concerns: multiline string quoting, indentation sensitivity, `$$` escaping conventions

==== What Survives

* `zrbf_stitch_build_json()` — continues to emit JSON via `jq`, adapted for trigger structure
* `rbgjb/*.sh` step scripts — inlined into JSON `args` arrays at inscribe time
* All `_RBGY_*` substitution variables — baked into JSON `substitutions` object
* OCI Layout Bridge pattern — unchanged

==== Provenance Note

SLSA v1.0 provenance records `buildConfigSource.path` which will contain `cloudbuild.json`.
This is a cosmetic difference from `cloudbuild.yaml` — the structured provenance fields
(build inputs, parameters, outputs) are identical regardless of config file format.

==== References

* Research conducted: 2026-02-24 (web search + API documentation review)
* Google docs consistently present YAML examples by convention, not by requirement
* The `gcloud builds submit` CLI internally converts any config format to JSON for API submission

==== Capture Composition Contract (rblds_ spine)

This section governs the host-composed `projects.builds.create` path for Lode
captures — distinct from the trigger-autodetect path the Decision above covers.
A Lode capture is composed on the host by the capture-assembly spine
(`rblds_Spine.sh`) from two data inputs handed by a per-kind body (e.g. bole's
`zrbld_ensconce_submit`): an ordered _recipe_ of step rows and an opaque
_substitutions_ blob. The spine carries no per-kind branch and no
kind-registration mechanism — a new Lode kind is a new body file the spine
consumes as data, never a conditional inside the spine.

===== Spine/body boundary

[cols="1,3",options="header"]
|===
|Owner |Carries

|Spine (`rblds_`)
|The step-composition loop (recipe rows -> `steps[]`); the Build-resource
envelope shape (`serviceAccount`, `options.automapSubstitutions`,
`options.logging`, `options.pool`, `timeout`); submit + build-id persist +
console link; the poll via `zrbfc_wait_build_completion`; and the
`buildStepOutputs[N]` base64-decode. The capture-domain constants — the mason
capture SA, the `TETHER` pool, the regime build timeout — are spine-owned,
because every Lode capture fetches upstream bytes as mason over the network.

|Body (`rbld«kind»_`)
|The recipe rows (pre-resolved); the substitutions blob; the poll ceiling (the
one kind-flavored build knob); and the interpretation of the decoded step
output (the per-member envelope fan-out).
|===

The only kind-flavored build knob the body passes is the poll ceiling (ensconce
borrows the enshrine ceiling). `serviceAccount`/`pool`/`timeout` are NOT body
parameters: they are capture-domain invariants the spine owns. A future kind
that needed a different pool or SA would change the spine's capture-domain
constants deliberately, not slip past as recipe data.

===== Recipe row format

A recipe is an ordered list of `|`-delimited step rows. The `|` delimiter is
load-bearing: builder image refs carry colons (tags and `sha256:` digests), so a
colon delimiter would be ambiguous.

----
script_path | builder_image | id | entrypoint
----

[cols="1,3",options="header"]
|===
|Field |Meaning

|`script_path`   |Absolute path to the step script, _pre-resolved by the body_. The spine owns no steps-directory knowledge.
|`builder_image` |The step's builder image ref (a reliquary tool image).
|`id`            |The Cloud Build step id.
|`entrypoint`    |`bash` \| `sh` \| `python3` — selects the composed shebang line. Any other value fails composition.
|===

The spine reads each script's body (shebang stripped), expands any
`#@rbgjs_include` markers against the shared snippet library (see _Composed-snippet
library_ below), prepends the shebang the entrypoint selects, and inlines the
result as the step's `script` field. This is the same shape the made-side
`rbfca_` assemblers use; the capture spine is the canonical single copy for the
fetched side.

===== Substitutions blob — opaque

The body composes the substitutions object (the `_RBGL_*` keys) into a file; the
spine slots it verbatim into the Build resource's `substitutions` field and
reads no key from it. `options.automapSubstitutions` maps the keys into each
step's environment. The spine's opacity to the blob is what keeps it
registration-agnostic — a new kind authors a different blob without touching the
spine.

Invariant (RBSCB): substitutions and `/workspace` carry non-secret data only;
secrets are step-local and re-minted.

===== Extract contract

The spine's extract decodes one step's `buildStepOutputs[N]` slot — base64 to a
destination file — where `N` is the only generic parameter. The decoded JSON's
shape (for bole: `slot_1`..`slot_N` member envelopes, each a stamp + vouch) is
the body's knowledge; the spine is member-count-agnostic. This keeps the
N-member envelope axis out of the spine.

===== Requires/provides format (designed here; enforced by the recipe validator)

The recipe row above is the format implemented today. The capture domain also
needs a per-step _requires/provides_ declaration so a dispatch-time validator
can reject a bad composition before the expensive build. The FORMAT is fixed
here; its enforcement is the recipe-validator pace.

A step composes over two register channels:

* _Substitution registers_ — `_RBGL_*` keys present in the substitutions blob,
  automapped into the step environment.
* _/workspace registers_ — non-secret files a step writes for a later step,
  named by path relative to `/workspace`.

Each step declares two token lists:

* `requires` — substitution-register keys and `/workspace` register paths the
  step reads before it runs.
* `provides` — `/workspace` register paths the step writes (and, for the
  terminal capture step, the `buildStepOutputs` slot it authors).

The validator walks the recipe in order: every `requires` token must be
satisfied by either a key present in the substitutions blob or a `provides`
token from an earlier step. An unsatisfiable `requires` fails dispatch with a
named-token diagnostic, before submit.

The format is deliberately shaped by the capture domain's three axes:

* _N-member envelopes_ — the terminal step's `provides` is the single
  `buildStepOutputs` slot, not per-member registers; member fan-out is body
  knowledge and stays out of the step contract.
* _Tool variety_ — registers are declared identically regardless of builder
  image or entrypoint, so a `python3` step and a `bash` step speak the same
  contract vocabulary.
* _Opaque-blob payloads_ — `requires` references substitution keys by NAME; the
  validator checks key _presence_, never value shape, preserving the spine's
  opacity to the blob.

31	- **RBGO** → `rbk/rbgo_OAuth.sh` (also owns the stateless `rbgo_curl_status_is_transient_predicate` transient-curl-exit classifier)
32	- **RBGP** → `rbk/rbgp_Payor.sh`
33	- **RBGV** → `rbk/rbgv_AccessProbe.sh` (Google Verification — JWT SA and Payor OAuth access probes)
34	- **RBH0** → `rbk/rbh0/` (Handbook directory — decomposed one-function-per-file)
35	  - `rbh*` is the Handbook family (human-facing procedures), parallel to `rbg*` (Google machinery). Three groups: `RBHO` onboarding, `RBHP` payor, `RBHW` windows. Colophon pattern: `rbw-h0` (HandbookTOP index), `rbw-h{o,p,w}` (group tops), `rbw-H{O,P,W}*` (subordinate procedures).
36	  - **RBHO0** → `rbk/rbh0/rbho0_cli.sh` (Onboarding CLI — thin furnish, probe-only dependencies)
37	  - **RBHOB** → `rbk/rbh0/rbhob_base.sh` (Onboarding base — kindle, sentinel, probes, shared helpers)
38	  - **RBHP0** → `rbk/rbh0/rbhp0_cli.sh` (Payor CLI — full regime + OAuth + IAM dependencies)
39	  - **RBHPB** → `rbk/rbh0/rbhpb_base.sh` (Payor base — kindle, sentinel, enforce)
40	  - **RBHW0** → `rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_cli.sh` (Windows CLI — handbook display + zipper dependencies)
41	  - **RBHWB** → `rbk/rbh0/rbhwb_base.sh` (Windows base — kindle, sentinel)

content.text:
All green. Here's where ₢BXAAE (cloud-step-library) stands.

## What landed

**New shared home — `Tools/rbk/rbgjs/`** (the no-family member of the `rbgj«family»/` cloud-step scheme), four composed-once snippets:
| Snippet | requires → provides | Live callers in-heat |
|---|---|---|
| `token-fetch` | — → `TOKEN` | 1 (`rbgjl01`; enshrine not migrated) |
| `skopeo-fingerprint` | `ORIGIN`,`RAW_FILE` → `SHA`,`FINGERPRINT` | 1 (`rbgjl01`) |
| `buildx-bootstrap` | — → `rb-builder` | 2 (`rbgjl02`,`rbgjv03`) |
| `buildx-push` | `PUSH_URI`,`PUSH_PLATFORMS`,`PUSH_CTX` → pushed image | 2 (`rbgjl02`,`rbgjv03`) |

**Mechanism** — `#@rbgjs_include «name»` markers, spliced at host-assembly by an **additive** `zrbfc_expand_includes` in `rbfcb_BuildHost.sh` (sibling to `write_script_body`, no existing logic touched), reached by both the Lode spine and the made-side vouch assembler. Snippets read shell vars the kind sets before the marker — never `_RBGx_` substitutions — which is what lets one snippet serve `_RBGL_*` and `_RBGV_*` callers. Unknown include → crash-fast, fails dispatch before submit.

**The made-side touch was surgical**: one `expand` call in `zrbfc_assemble_vouch_steps`; only `rbgjv03` carries markers (`v01`/`v02` pass through as no-ops).

**Recorded**: a "Composed-snippet library (rbgjs)" subsection in RBSCJ (the share-the-primitive resolution, the register channel, the four contracts, the partial-dedup reach); RBGJS mint in the acronym map.

## Verification
- `bash -n` clean on all 11 touched files; **shellcheck 200 files clean** (the 4 snippets now linted — the global SC2034/SC2154 suppressions cover the requires/provides fragment pattern).
- **Offline composition proof**: the real primitives compose each step; every diff vs pre-refactor HEAD is an intended delta (snippet docs inlined, two immaterial fatal-path message changes, `FINGERPRINT_TAG`→`FINGERPRINT`) — the capture/digest/fingerprint/push *logic* is byte-identical. Negative test confirms crash-fast on a bad include.
- **Live lode-lifecycle + conjure cook** (artifacts + provenance verdicts identical) needs GCP creds + standing depot — deferred to the heat gate per the paddock's verification model.

## Two judgment calls worth your eye
1. **Skopeo snippet narrowed to inspect→digest→fingerprint** (no first-copy), against the docket's guess — both kinds' first-copy destination is digest-derived per-kind, so it can't be a clean shared parameter.
2. **Snippet doc-comments ship into the build script** (~10–15 inert comment lines per include). Harmless and self-documenting in build logs, but verbose; a CBG-era call if you'd rather lean the build scripts.

Nothing committed — no notch yet. The work is done and statically verified; the live cook is the heat-gate item. Want me to **notch** ₢BXAAE's work, and is this where you'd consider wrapping it (I won't wrap without your say-so)?

message.content: Advise me on the judgement calls please.

content.text:
Here's my genuine read on each — recommendation first, then why, then how sure I am.

## #1 — Skopeo snippet boundary: **keep it narrowed. I'm confident.**

The seam I drew isn't arbitrary — it's a real semantic boundary: **measurement vs placement.** `inspect → digest → fingerprint` is pure measurement: deterministic, creds-free, multi-line, and the error-prone part worth getting right once. The first copy is *placement*: it puts bytes somewhere, and that "somewhere" is digest-derived and kind-specific (`${PKG}:${_RBGL_TAG_DIGEST_PREFIX}${SHA}` for Lode, `${DEST_REF}` built from the anchor for enshrine).

Including the copy can't be done cleanly: its destination depends on `SHA`, which the snippet computes — so the kind can't pre-set the destination before the include. You'd have to split into a second `skopeo-copy` snippet, and that one immediately fights the retag divergence (Lode's first copy is dest-creds-only; its retag loop is *both*-creds; enshrine is dest-only). So you'd buy ~1 line of dedup at the cost of a creds parameter and a second snippet.

And the clincher: enshrine isn't migrated, so sharing the copy would have **one** live caller in-heat — pure forward speculation, exactly what the paddock says not to do. Measurement-shared, placement-per-kind is the honest line.

## #2 — Snippet docs in the build script: **I recommend stripping the header on splice. Moderately confident — but it's partly your aesthetic call.**

I undersold this when I flagged it as "harmless verbosity." Re-reading it, there's a sharper problem than bloat: the headers are **self-referential and become false once inlined.** A composed build step ends up containing the line `Composed, not standalone — spliced into a step via #@rbgjs_include token-fetch` — sitting inside the step that has *manifestly already been spliced*. That's a small lie in the build artifact, and the CMK posture is to not ship those.

Three ways to resolve it:

| Option | Build script | Snippet file docs | Expander change |
|---|---|---|---|
| (a) leave as-is | verbose + self-contradictory | rich | none |
| (b) **strip header on splice** | lean + honest | **rich (file-only)** | ~4 lines |
| (c) trim headers to one-liner | modestly lean + honest | thin | none |

I recommend **(b)**. It's the only one that keeps the snippet *files* richly documented — which is what the paddock explicitly wants (snippets are the CBG exemplars; their contracts should teach) — while the *build* gets only logic plus the genuinely-useful inline comments. The rule is clean and well-defined: after the shebang, skip the leading contiguous comment/blank block, splice from the first code line. I checked it against all four snippets — it strips exactly the doc-header and **preserves** the inline logic comments (`# sha256sum (coreutils…)`, `# Sanitize origin…`), which are good build-log context. And each kind already keeps its own one-line "what" comment before the include, so the build log doesn't go dark.

(c) is the cheapest but trades away the file documentation, which is the wrong direction given the exemplar goal.

Why only *moderately* confident: (b) adds a touch of expander complexity for a log-only benefit, and a reasonable person could call that gold-plating and pick (a). The self-contradiction is what tips me past YAGNI — it's a correctness-of-meaning fix, not just cosmetics. It's also fully reversible and cheap to defer to CBG-harvest time if you'd rather not touch the expander again now.

If you want **(b)**, I'll add the header-strip to `zrbfc_expand_includes` and re-run the offline proof (the composed bodies will then drop the doc-headers — I'll confirm the diffs show only that).

lastPrompt: Advise me on the judgement calls please.

message.content: Hmm, I am quite uncomfortable with adding 'code edit' to the process of assembling the script.  Wy are you suggesting this?

content.text:
You're right, and the discomfort is the correct signal — I'll withdraw (b).

**Why it's wrong:** the expander's job is *composition* — splice text verbatim. Option (b) would turn it into a *content editor*: it'd have to parse each snippet, classify "this comment block is a header, strip it / this comment is inline, keep it," and guess intent via a "leading contiguous comment block" heuristic. That's fragile (what about a blank line in the header, or a snippet that legitimately opens with an inline comment?) and it's a category change from "dumb splicer" to "editorial transformer." Worse, it quietly weakens the verification: the offline proof is *powerful precisely because the splice is verbatim* — I can diff the composed body and see the logic is byte-identical with the only deltas being literal snippet text. The moment the assembler edits content, that clean property is gone and I'd be verifying the stripping logic itself.

**Why I reached for it anyway** — and this is the honest answer to your question: I correctly spotted a real problem (the self-referential header reads as false once inlined) but grabbed the *nearest* mechanism (the expander) instead of asking *which layer owns this concern*. And I let a false precedent lull me — "the expander already strips the shebang, so stripping a header is just more of the same." That equivalence is wrong: the shebang-strip is **structural** (a shebang is a positional interpreter directive with exactly one correct treatment), not **editorial**. I conflated removing one structurally-special line with removing an arbitrary-length block I'd classify by judgment. That's the slip — optimizing for the output I wanted (lean build) and corrupting a clean abstraction to get it.

**Where the concern actually belongs, if anywhere:** authoring time, not assembly time. If the inlined header bothers us, the fix is to *write the header so it's true in both places* — drop the "Composed, not standalone — spliced via `#@rbgjs_include`…" and "shebang exists only for shellcheck" lines (the meta that only makes sense pre-splice), keep the substantive "what + requires/provides." That's an edit to four files I already wrote; the expander stays a pure verbatim splicer. Or just leave the headers as-is and accept the inline verbosity.

My recommendation now: **keep the expander purely verbatim** (this is the load-bearing call, and you're right to hold it), and treat the headers as a minor authoring question — I'd lightly trim the four to remove the self-referential lines, but it's small and (a) leave-as-is is entirely defensible. I won't push it either way. Which do you want?

message.content: You are allowed to author these scripts, in fact it is part of this pace.  But do not complicate the assembly process.  proceed.

content.text: Understood — author the snippets (that's in-scope), keep the assembler dead-simple verbatim. The expander stays exactly as it is (verbatim splice; the shebang-strip is the one *structural* exception and it stays). I'll fix this purely at the authoring layer: trim the four headers so every line is true whether read in the file or inlined — dropping the self-referential "spliced via…" / "shebang exists only for shellcheck" meta (that mechanism doc already lives in RBSCJ), keeping the substantive description + requires/provides.

input.content:
#!/bin/bash
# RBGJS token-fetch — OAuth2 access token from the Cloud Build metadata server
# (Mason SA). The canonical skopeo-to-GAR authentication (RBSCB): skopeo cannot
# use the gcloud credential helper, so the token is fetched in-memory and never
# written to /workspace, which carries non-secret data only.
#   requires: (none — ambient metadata server)
#   provides: TOKEN
echo "Fetching OAuth2 token from metadata server"
TOKEN_JSON=$(curl -sf -H "Metadata-Flavor: Google" \
  "http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/instance/service-accounts/default/token") \
  || { echo "Failed to fetch OAuth2 token from metadata server" >&2; exit 1; }

TOKEN=$(printf '%s' "${TOKEN_JSON}" | grep -o '"access_token":"[^"]*"' | cut -d'"' -f4)
test -n "${TOKEN}" || { echo "Failed to extract access_token from metadata response" >&2; exit 1; }

#!/bin/bash
# RBGJS snippet: OAuth2 access token from the Cloud Build metadata server (Mason
# SA). Composed, not standalone — spliced into a step via
#   #@rbgjs_include token-fetch
# The shebang exists only so shellcheck lints this fragment as bash; the include
# expander strips it on splice. The fragment runs inside the kind's step body,
# which owns `set -euo pipefail`.
#
# The metadata-server token is the canonical skopeo-to-GAR authentication
# (RBSCB): skopeo cannot use the gcloud credential helper, so the token is
# fetched in-memory and never written to /workspace — which carries non-secret
# data only.
#
#   requires: (none — ambient metadata server, parameter-free)
#   provides: TOKEN  (shell var — OAuth2 access token)
echo "Fetching OAuth2 token from metadata server"
TOKEN_JSON=$(curl -sf -H "Metadata-Flavor: Google" \
  "http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/instance/service-accounts/default/token") \
  || { echo "Failed to fetch OAuth2 token from metadata server" >&2; exit 1; }

TOKEN=$(printf '%s' "${TOKEN_JSON}" | grep -o '"access_token":"[^"]*"' | cut -d'"' -f4)
test -n "${TOKEN}" || { echo "Failed to extract access_token from metadata response" >&2; exit 1; }

#!/bin/bash
# RBGJS skopeo-fingerprint — inspect an upstream image's raw manifest, take its
# canonical sha256 digest, and derive the sanitized-origin glance fingerprint.
# The canonical digest is the sha256 of the RAW manifest bytes (what every tool
# reports); the fingerprint is the sanitized origin (`:` and `/` become `-`) plus
# the first 10 hex of that digest — the legacy enshrine anchor form. Only this
# inspect+digest+fingerprint core is identical across the skopeo captures
# (ensconce, enshrine); the first GAR copy is kind-specific (its destination tag
# is itself digest-derived) and stays in the step.
#   requires: ORIGIN    upstream image ref (e.g. docker.io/library/x:tag)
#             RAW_FILE  /workspace path to write the raw manifest into
#   provides: SHA          64-hex canonical digest (no algorithm prefix)
#             FINGERPRINT  "<sanitized-origin>-<first-10-hex>"
skopeo inspect --raw "docker://${ORIGIN}" > "${RAW_FILE}" \
  || { echo "FATAL: Failed to inspect upstream: ${ORIGIN}" >&2; exit 1; }

# sha256sum (coreutils, not openssl) — runs inside the skopeo reliquary container.
SHA=$(sha256sum "${RAW_FILE}" | cut -d' ' -f1)
test -n "${SHA}" || { echo "FATAL: Empty digest for ${ORIGIN}" >&2; exit 1; }

# Sanitize origin (: and / become -), append first 10 hex chars (legacy anchor form).
FINGERPRINT="$(printf '%s' "${ORIGIN}" | tr ':/' '--')-${SHA:0:10}"

#!/bin/bash
# RBGJS buildx-bootstrap — ensure the shared docker-container buildx builder
# exists and is selected. Idempotent under Cloud Build retry: inspect-or-create,
# then use. Run once per step before any push; safe to re-run (a second inspect
# succeeds, create is skipped, use is a no-op).
#   requires: (none)
#   provides: the "rb-builder" buildx builder, created if absent and selected
docker buildx inspect rb-builder >/dev/null 2>&1 \
  || docker buildx create --driver docker-container --name rb-builder
docker buildx use rb-builder

#!/bin/bash
# RBGJS buildx-push — push a prepared FROM-scratch build context as an image via
# the shared buildx builder (see buildx-bootstrap). The irreducible push shared
# by the FROM-scratch vouch-push callers (Lode rbgjl02, hallmark-verify rbgjv03):
# push cardinality, the destination URI, the platform set, and the context
# assembly (which JSON, conditional Dockerfile) are all done by the kind.
#   requires: PUSH_URI        full destination image ref including tag
#             PUSH_PLATFORMS  buildx --platform value (e.g. linux/amd64)
#             PUSH_CTX        build context dir holding the Dockerfile
#   provides: the image pushed to PUSH_URI
docker buildx build \
  --push \
  --platform="${PUSH_PLATFORMS}" \
  --tag "${PUSH_URI}" \
  "${PUSH_CTX}" \
  || { echo "FATAL: buildx push failed for ${PUSH_URI}" >&2; exit 1; }

#!/bin/bash
# RBGJS snippet: push a prepared FROM-scratch build context as an image via the
# shared buildx builder. Composed, not standalone — spliced into a step via
#   #@rbgjs_include buildx-push
# The shebang exists only so shellcheck lints this fragment as bash; the include
# expander strips it on splice.
#
# This is the irreducible push shared by the FROM-scratch vouch-push callers
# (Lode rbgjl02, hallmark-verify rbgjv03). Everything kind-specific — how many
# times you push (Lode loops N members; verify pushes once), the destination URI,
# the platform set (Lode pins linux/amd64; verify reads a platforms file), and
# the context assembly (which JSON, conditional Dockerfile) — is done by the kind
# before the include. Requires the builder from buildx-bootstrap.
#
#   requires: PUSH_URI        (shell var — full destination image ref including tag)
#             PUSH_PLATFORMS  (shell var — buildx --platform value, e.g. linux/amd64)
#             PUSH_CTX        (shell var — build context dir holding the Dockerfile)
#             (the rb-builder builder — see buildx-bootstrap)
#   provides: the image pushed to PUSH_URI
docker buildx build \
  --push \
  --platform="${PUSH_PLATFORMS}" \
  --tag "${PUSH_URI}" \
  "${PUSH_CTX}" \
  || { echo "FATAL: buildx push failed for ${PUSH_URI}" >&2; exit 1; }

Composition rides a new verbatim splicer zrbfc_expand_includes (rbfcb_BuildHost.sh, sibling to write_script_body, additive — no existing logic touched): it replaces '#@rbgjs_include <name>' markers with rbgjs-<name>.sh (shebang stripped), a no-op for marker-free bodies, crash-fast on an unknown name. Deliberately kept a dumb verbatim splicer — assembly does NO content editing; the inlined-header concern was solved at authoring time, not in the assembler. Wired into the Lode spine (rblds_, every step) and the made-side vouch assembler (rbfca_ zrbfc_assemble_vouch_steps — the one sanctioned made-side touch; only rbgjv03 carries markers). ZRBFC_RBGJS_SNIPPETS_DIR added to rbfck_Kindle.sh beside the RBGJV/RBGJR step dirs.

Snippets read shell vars the kind sets before the marker, never _RBGx_ substitutions — what lets one snippet serve disjoint substitution namespaces (_RBGL_* vs _RBGV_*). rbgjl01 rides token-fetch + skopeo-fingerprint (keeping per-kind DIGEST_TAG/PKG/first-copy/retag-loop/envelope); rbgjl02 and rbgjv03 ride buildx-bootstrap + buildx-push.

Resolved the OPEN docket challenge against the bytes (operator-cinched share-the-primitive): vouch-push is NOT one whole shared step — rbgjl02 and rbgjv03 diverge on five load-bearing axes (cardinality, URI template/arity, platform source, conditional Dockerfile, disjoint substitutions); only the buildx bootstrap + push invocation are identical, so only those are shared. The same measurement-vs-placement boundary narrowed the skopeo snippet to inspect->digest->fingerprint, excluding the first GAR copy since its destination tag is digest-derived per-kind. Partial dedup: bootstrap/push have two live callers; token-fetch/skopeo-fingerprint have one (enshrine reference-not-migrated).

Recorded the Composed-snippet library (rbgjs) contract in RBSCJ (mechanism, shell-var register channel, the four contracts, share-the-primitive resolution) and refreshed the recipe-row paragraph to note the include pass; minted RBGJS in the acronym map.

Verification: bash -n clean (11 files), shellcheck 200 files clean (4 new snippets), and an offline composition proof — the real host primitives compose rbgjl01/rbgjl02/rbgjv03 to valid bash with diffs vs pre-refactor HEAD showing only intended deltas (snippet text, two immaterial fatal-path messages, FINGERPRINT_TAG->FINGERPRINT); capture/digest/fingerprint/push logic byte-identical; unknown-include crash-fast confirmed. Live lode-lifecycle + conjure-vouch cook (artifacts + provenance verdicts identical) deferred to the heat gate per the paddock verification model and the BXAAD precedent."}

content.text:
Notch landed clean — commit `aadfed902`, all **13 files** in it (the 4 `rbgjs/` snippets + 9 modified, 224 insertions), tree now clean. That `?? Tools/rbk/rbgjs/` warning was a pre-staging snapshot; `jjx_record` then staged the explicitly-listed new files. Verified above.

## Wrap candidacy for ₢BXAAE

**My advice: it's a legitimate wrap candidate — but only with an honest, cook-deferred summary.** Here's the reasoning, including the one thing that gives me pause.

**Against the docket's "Done", four of five lines are met:**
- ✅ shared home minted + recorded (rbgjs/, RBSCJ, acronym map)
- ✅ Lode + hallmark vouch-push ride the shared library (refined to share-the-primitive)
- ✅ token + copy/digest composed once (skopeo principledly narrowed to the measurement core)
- ✅ each snippet's requires/provides declared
- ❌ **"lode-lifecycle and a conjure path green with verdicts identical to pre-refactor"** — the live cook. Not done in-chat; needs GCP creds + a standing depot.

**Why deferring that last line is consistent, not a dodge:** the paddock's verification model explicitly makes the ~100-minute skirmish/service cook a *heat-gate milestone*, not a per-pace cost — "the heat pays one full cook, at the gate." And the precedent is right next door: ₢BXAAD (the spine pace) wrapped with "live lode-lifecycle/provenance confirmation deferred to the heat gate per the paddock verification model and operator decision." The per-pace bar is static verification, and mine is fully green (bash -n, shellcheck 200 clean, offline composition proof, crash-fast negative test).

**The caveat I want you to weigh — this pace broadens the gate's debt beyond what the spine pace owed.** ₢BXAAD was fetched-side only. ₢BXAAE is the **first capture pace to touch the made-side**: `rbgjv03` plus the conjure-vouch assembler (`zrbfc_assemble_vouch_steps` now routes through the expander). So the heat gate now owes confirmation on *two* live paths, not one:
1. **lode-lifecycle (ensconce)** — fetched-side, already owed since the spine.
2. **a conjure/bind/graft vouch** — made-side, newly owed by this pace.

Both must vouch identically to pre-refactor. The offline proof shows `rbgjv03` composes correctly and its logic is byte-identical, and the failure modes are all crash-fast (the kindle has `test -d … || buc_die`; an unset snippets-dir makes the expander `return 1` → `buc_die`) — so nothing here can silently corrupt a verdict; a wiring slip would fail loudly at the gate's first cook. That's why I'm comfortable it's deferrable rather than blocking.

**Recommendation:**
- **Wrap-eligible: yes.** If you wrap, the `jjx_close` summary should mirror ₢BXAAD's honesty — state the live lode-lifecycle **and** conjure-vouch cook is deferred to the heat gate, not imply the Done's cook line is satisfied.
- **One note worth carrying to the gate** (not a pre-wrap blocker): because of the made-side touch, the gate must exercise a *conjure-vouch*, not just lode-lifecycle — otherwise this pace's made-side blast radius goes unconfirmed.

Optional, if you'd rather not lean entirely on the gate: I can run a runtime kindle smoke of the foundry furnish chain to confirm `ZRBFC_RBGJS_SNIPPETS_DIR` wires and the made-side assembler reaches the expander — but it may need regime/creds to kindle, and the failure modes are already crash-fast, so it's low-value. Your call whether to wrap now or smoke first.

lastPrompt: Is it appropriate to attempt 'dogfight' instead of full 'skirmish' or 'gauntlet' now?

content.text:
Read the fixtures — here's a precise answer, because the suites map cleanly onto what this pace touched.

## What each fixture actually exercises

- **`dogfight`** (`rbtdrd_build_run_lifecycle`): ordains busybox **in conjure mode** → summon → bare `docker run` → abjure. The conjure build runs the full vouch pipeline (`rbgjv01/02/03` via `zrbfc_assemble_vouch_steps`), and summon SLSA-verifies the vouch. So it exercises the **made-side conjure-vouch path** — `rbgjv03` plus the vouch-assembler expander wiring. It charges **no crucible** and **reuses a standing depot** (no levy/unmake), so it's materially cheaper than skirmish.
- **`lode-lifecycle`** (`rbtdrc_lode_lifecycle`): ensconce a Lode → divine-enumerate → divine-inspect "the member tags + vouch envelope rode in" → banish. That's the **fetched-side Lode ensconce path** — `rbgjl01`/`rbgjl02` riding token-fetch, skopeo-fingerprint, and the buildx snippets.
- **`skirmish`** = the "mini-gauntlet" depot→build→crucible chain (charges a crucible; ~100 min per the paddock). **`gauntlet`** = the full release-qualification ladder. Both are much heavier.

## My answer

**Yes — dogfight is appropriate now, and it's well-aimed at this pace's *new* risk — but it's a half-gate.** This pace touched two paths, and they split exactly across two fixtures:

| This pace's touch | Confirmed by |
|---|---|
| made-side conjure-vouch (`rbgjv03` + vouch-assembler expander) — **new this pace** | **dogfight** |
| fetched-side Lode ensconce (`rbgjl01`/`rbgjl02` + snippets) | **lode-lifecycle** |

So:
- **dogfight alone** confirms the made-side half — the genuinely-new blast radius this pace introduced (the spine pace was fetched-side only). A green dogfight is strong, cheap evidence that conjure-vouch is verdict-identical post-refactor. That's the highest-value thing to confirm early, and yes — far more appropriate than paying for full skirmish/gauntlet right now.
- **But dogfight does *not* touch the Lode ensconce path.** Your snippet changes to `rbgjl01`/`rbgjl02` go unverified by dogfight.

**Sharpest recommendation:** if you want to live-confirm *this pace* without the full cook, run the targeted pair **dogfight + lode-lifecycle** (`tt/rbw-tf.FixtureRun.sh` for each). They cover exactly the two paths the pace touched, at a fraction of skirmish's cost. dogfight is the right *first* move (new/riskier half); lode-lifecycle closes the fetched-side half.

**Two honesties:**
1. Operational precondition — dogfight needs a **standing depot already levied + director/retriever invested + GCP creds + local docker** (for the run-proof). It won't create a depot. If that's provisioned, it's cheap; if not, it isn't free.
2. Even green, **dogfight + lode-lifecycle is targeted-pace confirmation, not the paddock's designated heat gate** (full skirmish + service lode-lifecycle, which also re-walks the broader depot→build→crucible chain). It de-risks the gate early — it doesn't discharge it.

If you want, I can kick off `dogfight` now (I'd need you to confirm the standing depot + creds are in place), and we read the verdict before deciding on lode-lifecycle.

content.text:
Diagnosed precisely. Here's what happened — and it gives us **zero signal on ₢BXAAE either way**, because my code never ran.

## Where it died: suite setup, not the fixture

The credential re-provisioning chain ran: governor-mantle ✓, director-divest ✓, retriever-divest ✓, retriever-invest ✓ — then **`rbtdrk_director_invest` failed**. That step got all the way through SA creation, key mint, and RBRA write, and failed on the *last* action:

```
Adding Cloud Build Editor role (project scope)
ERROR: HTTP request failed after 3 attempts (curl exit 28)
```

**curl exit 28 = operation timeout.** The IAM role-binding REST call to GCP timed out, three times. The dogfight *fixture* (`rbtdrd_build_run_lifecycle` — ordain busybox conjure → summon) **never started**. The conjure-vouch path — `rbgjv03` and the expander wiring, the whole reason to run dogfight — was never reached.

## Read: this is the Pale, not a ₢BXAAE regression

This is a transient GCP IAM/network timeout during *credential setup* — foreign-environment behavior, precisely signed (curl 28, not "flaky"), and entirely upstream of the cloud-step snippet library I touched. Nothing in this pace affects governor→IAM service-account provisioning. So:
- It is **not** evidence the refactor is broken.
- It is **not** evidence the refactor is sound either — we got no coverage of my code.

One side effect to note: director-invest created the SA and wrote `rbra.env` before timing out, so the director SA likely exists *without* the Cloud Build Editor binding. The suite's divest/re-invest setup is re-runnable, so a fresh run cleans that up.

## I'm re-running it

A single transient IAM timeout is the most likely cause, and the obvious move is to retry — so I'm re-launching dogfight now rather than round-trip for confirmation (this run delivered no signal, and the re-run directly serves the goal). If it fails again at the *same* IAM call, that's a GCP-side issue (endpoint/propagation/quota) for you to chase — still not pace code.

Dogfight is **fully green — 6/6**, and crucially the fixture itself passed, not just setup:

```
PASSED: rbtdrk_governor_mantle / director_divest / retriever_divest / retriever_invest / director_invest   (5/5 setup)
PASSED: rbtdrd_build_run_lifecycle   (~390s — the real conjure Cloud Build)
Suite 'dogfight': 2 fixture(s) run, 6 passed, 0 failed, 0 skipped
```

(The payor reauth cleared the connectivity wall — both prior failures were the curl-28 timeouts, never my code.)

## What this confirms for ₢BXAAE — live

`rbtdrd_build_run_lifecycle` is **ordain busybox (conjure) → summon → run → abjure**. That path runs:
- the conjure Cloud Build, whose vouch step is the **refactored `rbgjv03`** — composed through the now-expander-wired `zrbfc_assemble_vouch_steps`, riding the `buildx-bootstrap` + `buildx-push` snippets. The build succeeded, so the composed vouch step **assembled and executed in a real GCP Cloud Build container**.
- **summon**, which runs the SLSA vouch gate on that artifact. It passed — so the vouch the refactored `rbgjv03` pushed **verifies correctly**. That's precisely the heat's behavior-preservation criterion (provenance *verdict* identical, not bytes) — confirmed live for the conjure path.
- the `docker run` executability proof.

So the **made-side blast radius this pace newly introduced is confirmed live**: the expander works end-to-end in the real assembly path, and the conjure-vouch verdict survives the refactor. This is the part I flagged as the genuinely-new risk, and it's now green on live GCP.

## What's still open

Dogfight does **not** touch the fetched-side Lode ensconce — `rbgjl01`/`rbgjl02` riding `token-fetch`, `skopeo-fingerprint`, and the buildx snippets. That's the **`lode-lifecycle`** fixture (`tt/rbw-tf.FixtureRun.sh lode-lifecycle`): ensconce a Lode → divine-inspect that member tags + vouch envelope rode in → banish. It's service-tier (needs the now-live creds + standing depot, no crucible).

## Where that leaves the pace

| Check | Status |
|---|---|
| static (bash -n, shellcheck 200, offline composition proof) | ✅ |
| live made-side conjure-vouch (dogfight) | ✅ **new** |
| live fetched-side Lode ensconce (lode-lifecycle) | ⏳ pending |

Running `lode-lifecycle` next would give **complete live coverage of both paths this pace touched** — stronger than the ₢BXAAD precedent, which deferred everything to the heat gate. With that green, wrapping ₢BXAAE would rest on live confirmation of its full blast radius, not just static + deferral.

Want me to kick off `lode-lifecycle` now (creds + depot are warm)? Or hold here and treat the made-side green as sufficient, deferring the fetched-side to the heat gate?

content.text:
Wrapped — ₢BXAAE is closed (commit `0aaaa4be`, state-transition only since the code rode the earlier notch and the tree was clean).

## ₢BXAAE (cloud-step-library) — done

**Delivered:** the shared cloud-step snippet library `rbgjs/` (the no-family member of the `rbgj«family»/` scheme), four composed-once snippets, a deliberately-verbatim include-expander (`zrbfc_expand_includes`, additive), the share-the-primitive resolution of the vouch-push challenge, one sanctioned made-side touch (`rbgjv03`), and the contract recorded in RBSCJ + the mint in the acronym map.

**Verification — the full picture:**
| Check | Result |
|---|---|
| static (bash -n, shellcheck 200, offline composition proof, crash-fast negative) | ✅ |
| live made-side conjure-vouch — `dogfight` (ordain→summon SLSA-verify→run→abjure) | ✅ 6/6 |
| live fetched-side Lode ensconce — `lode-lifecycle` (ensconce→inspect tags+vouch→banish) | ✅ 1/1 |

Both paths this pace touched are live-confirmed verdict-identical — stronger than the ₢BXAAD precedent, which deferred all live checks to the heat gate. The full skirmish stays the heat-level milestone, not a pace blocker.

**Two things worth carrying forward:** the curl-28 timeouts were a payor/connectivity issue you reauthorized, never the code; and the include-expander is intentionally a dumb verbatim splicer — a good invariant to preserve as future Lode kinds attach.

## Next

The heat's next pace is **₢BXAAF (dispatch-recipe-validator)** — and it's a different animal: the heat's *one declared behavior-adding sliver*, genuinely new code with its own targeted unit tests (its reject-path is exercised by no current fixture). That's a fresh-context job — the JJK wrap response suggests `/clear` then `mount BX` when you're ready to take it on.

Nice work landing this one. Anything you want before you clear?
